prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 And behold there was set before thee a charge and a commandment saying Write thou a passage concerning that season of the year which is most beloved unto thine heart

1:2 And the manner of thy writing shall be thus that thou shalt employ no mark of punctuation whatsoever neither the full stop nor the mark of separation between clauses

1:3 Thou shalt set down no apostrophe within thy words nor shalt thou place any mark of questioning at the end of thine inquiries nor any mark of exclamation after thy declarations

1:4 Yea verily I say unto thee that no mark of punctuation of any kind soever shall appear in thy writing neither those which have been named nor any other marks which serve a like purpose

1:5 For this is the law and the commandment concerning thy composition that the words shall flow one unto another without ceasing and without the dividing marks which scribes are wont to place among their words

1:6 Go thou therefore and write concerning thy favored season and let thy pen move freely but let no punctuation dwell therein
response:
Chapter 2

2:1 And lo I shall speak unto thee of that most glorious season which is called autumn for it is the season most beloved unto mine heart and the time when the earth doth clothe herself in raiment of gold and crimson

2:2 For when the summer hath spent its fierce heat upon the land and the days do begin to shorten and the air doth grow cool upon the face of the morning then doth autumn come forth like a gentle sovereign to claim dominion over the fields and the forests

2:3 And the trees which stood in garments of green throughout the long days of summer do cast off their former apparel and put on robes of scarlet and amber and burnished copper such that the hillsides appear as though a great painter hath taken his brush unto them

2:4 The harvest doth come in from the fields and the orchards do yield up their fruits and the apples hang heavy upon the bough red and golden and the scent of them doth fill the air with sweetness

2:5 And in the mornings there cometh a mist upon the valleys and the dew doth lie thick upon the grass and the spider doth weave her web between the fence posts and the threads thereof do glisten like silver in the early light

2:6 The geese do gather themselves together in great companies and they fly across the heavens in formations like unto the shape of an arrow and their calling one to another doth echo across the sky as they journey southward

2:7 And the children do walk among the fallen leaves and the sound of their footsteps is as a gentle rustling and they do gather unto themselves the acorns and the chestnuts and the pine cones which the trees have let fall upon the ground

2:8 In the evening the sun doth set earlier than before and it doth paint the western sky in hues of purple and rose and the air doth carry upon it the smell of wood smoke from the hearths of those who have kindled their first fires of the season

2:9 And there is in autumn a certain stillness and a certain sweetness which belongeth to no other season for it is the time of reflection and of gratitude when the earth doth pause between the abundance of summer and the slumber of winter

2:10 Therefore I say unto thee that autumn is the crown of the year and the most beautiful of all the seasons and my heart doth rejoice whensoever its appointed time doth come again upon the land
